stories.title: The Thief-Taker's Daughter
stories.text:
Mira the Shadow-Dancer had stolen from the wrong trade-lord. The price was not her life but her father's—Aldric the locksmith, cursed decades ago to feel every lock he opened as a wound. Now the trade-lord offered reprieve: steal the sealed ledger from the Vault Archives in three nights, and Aldric's curse dissolves.

Mira moved through the Archives like smoke, past warded doors that Aldric's trembling fingers had designed. Each lock she bypassed sang with his old craftsmanship, a ghost of his better days. The ledger lay in the deepest vault, bound in shadowless leather.

But the trade-lord had lied. The curse was not dissolved—it was *transferred*. As Mira clutched the ledger, she felt every lock she'd ever picked ignite in her palms. Aldric wept with relief as his hands finally stilled. Mira screamed.

The trade-lord smiled. A new thief-taker in his service. Better than any coin.

Yet Mira grinned through her agony. She'd palmed a second ledger—the *real* one—during the exchange. This trade-lord had just hired a woman who could feel every guard he set against her. By dawn, the Archives would be emptied of secrets. By dusk, every crime in the city would be exposed.

Some curses, if you're clever, become your sharpest blade.

stories.title: The Bone-Singer's Vigil
stories.text: Kess of the Drowned Depths has not sung in three days. The blight-rot that once blackened the village is long buried, but the new stars overhead burn too bright for her comfort—she remembers the old ones, and the ancient hymns they demanded. Now she keeps watch at the Sunken Spring, listening for voices in the water that may not be wholly human anymore. The deep-dwellers have begun sending scouts inland, drawn by something in the ley-lines that Kess recognizes as hunger. She knows what they seek: the same thing the Stellar Rite left behind. A merchant caravan passing through the Flooded Marshes reports supplies going missing; Kess knows it is not theft. It is payment, being collected in advance. When a young woman arrives claiming to hear singing in her dreams, Kess must choose: teach her the old bone-songs to defend herself, or silence her before the water learns her name.

stories.title: The Ash-Caller's Debt
stories.text: In the Scorched Wastes, where trade has halted and caravans no longer pass, a hired blade named Morn discovers that the spice routes have not merely closed—they have been sealed by something that walks the sand. The merchants who vanished did not die; they were collected, their shadows harvested and sold to a figure who moves through the night markets of Kethrim wearing borrowed darkness. Morn tracks the ash-caller to an oasis where dead water reflects no stars, and there learns the truth: the trade-lord Vaston made a bargain with a demon years ago, trading the lives of travelers for certainty of profit. Now the demon is calling in old debts, and those who profited from death must choose between payment or fleeing into the new starlight that rewrites the sky. Morn must decide whether to free the sealed souls or claim the shadow-merchant's hoard for herself.

stories.title: The Cartographer's Silence
stories.text: Mardoc the Cartographer has stopped drawing maps. His hands shake when he holds a stylus, and the lines of the new constellation burn behind his eyes whenever he closes them. He knows what the pre-human ruins are aligned to—not a star-gate, but a doorway. And it is opening. In the Pallid Wastes, his expedition discovers that the ruins have begun to hum in unison, a frequency that makes teeth ache and blood weep from old scars. The Recluse finds him there, half-mad with fragmented memory, and together they realize the Stellar Rite was not a renewal—it was a summoning. Something is being invited home. Mardoc must choose whether to warn the Empire and face execution for knowledge he cannot fully explain, or remain silent and watch the doorways open. Either way, the ruins are waking, and they remember hunger.

stories.title: The Night-Market Reckoning
stories.text: Vex the Merchant has developed a new problem: her customers are disappearing into the crowds of Kethros's night markets, never to return. A young scholar from the capital arrives with strange reports—entire trading families have vanished without trace, leaving behind only their goods, stacked neatly in warehouses. Together they trace the pattern through the night markets, discovering that a single entity is luring merchants with promises of impossible profits, then erasing all memory of them from ledgers and witnesses alike. Vex realizes her own missing apprentice may be among them. The entity is not buying wares—it is collecting merchants themselves, their knowledge, their contacts, their networks. Vex must decide whether to use her intimate knowledge of the market underworld to set a trap, knowing it will destroy the only life she has ever known, or abandon her apprentice to preserve herself. Time is running short before the entity reaches the highest chambers of trade.

stories.title: The Forge-Master's Ruin
stories.text: Dranin Stoneheart has discovered that the new starlight corrodes his best work. Weapons forged in silver and moonstone—the finest craftsmanship of his long life—are turning to rust and ash, piece by piece. His apprentice Mella notices the pattern first: the corruption spreads from runes inscribed in the old tongue, as if the metal itself is rejecting the language that gave it strength. In the mountain holds of the dwarves, rumors speak of a curse affecting all stonecraft-magic, a subtle rejection spreading through every working touched by the old ley-lines. Dranin must travel to the Tower of Starwatch to confront the truth: the Stellar Rite did not merely rewrite the stars—it severed something fundamental between earth-magic and sky-magic. The dwarven holds are failing in silence, their foundations weakening day by day. He must choose between rallying his people to face the rift between worlds, or hoarding what resources remain and letting the holds fall one by one.

stories.title: The Warden's Council
stories.text: Sylas the Deepwarden has called an emergency convening of the Council of Depths. The barrier between the deep-dweller realms and the sunken waters is fracturing—not from external pressure, but from something calling from the other side. The spectral presence born from Zareth's sacrificed memories has reached the Abyssal Trench, and it is singing in the language of starlight. The deep-dwellers cannot understand it, but they can feel it pulling at their consciousness, offering fragments of memories that do not belong to them. Some of the younger deep-dwellers are volunteering to enter the singing waters, believing they hear their own lost names being called. Sylas must hold the Council together while deciding whether to seal the barrier—trapping whatever is on both sides—or to attempt communication with the entity. A delegation arrives from the Steel Coast, claiming refugees are fleeing inland from coastal villages where the water no longer reflects the sky. The Sunken Spring grows restless.

stories.title: The Young Sorcerer's Burden
stories.text: The Young Sorcerer has inherited the Grimoire of Endless Knowing, and within its pages lies a truth that cannot be unread: the Stellar Rite created not one entity but many, fragments of consciousness scattered across the rewritten world like seeds. Some are dormant. Others are very much awake. In the Tower hidden among the Scorched Lands, the sorcerer discovers that the magical ley-lines are converging on a single point—directly beneath the Tower itself. The ancient structure was built as a prison, and the Stellar Rite has weakened every lock. The sorcerer must descend into the depths where the Elder Mage walked, following passages carved before humanity learned language, seeking the source of the ley-line convergence. Down there, something is waiting. Something that remembers being imprisoned, and it is very grateful that the stars have been rewritten.

stories.title: The Archer's Cold Trail
stories.text: Ashara the Archer has been following rumors of a memory-thief moving through the western provinces, stealing recollections of the old stars from anyone who witnessed the Stellar Rite. The victims are left whole but hollow—they function, they breathe, but something essential has been excised. Ashara tracked one such victim to a village where rationing has begun, not from scarcity but from suspicion: no one can remember whether they paid for their food. Commerce is breaking down because contracts and debts are forgetting themselves. She discovers the thief is a woman wearing a borrowed face, moving between towns, collecting memories in sealed vials. When Ashara finally corners her, the woman speaks in a voice like wind through dead stars: "I am only remembering myself." The archer must decide whether to kill a creature that may not be fully responsible for its actions, or to help it find the scattered pieces of its own stolen identity—pieces that may rebuild it into something even more dangerous.

stories.title: The Druid's Covenant
stories.text: Deep in the ancient forests beyond the Verdant Lands, the circle-keepers have noticed that the beasts are forgetting their territories. Stags that have walked the same paths for generations are now lost, confused by ley-lines that no longer lead where they should. The seasonal magics are stuttering—spring arrives late, winter lingers in crevices, and the growing things are hungry in new ways. An old circle-keeper named Thael realizes that the Stellar Rite has broken the compact between earth and sky, the covenant that governed harvest and decay. She begins a counter-working, but it demands payment: she must give back what was taken. That means traveling to the Peak of Aethros where Zareth performed the Rite, retracing his steps, and offering something the forest holds dear. Along the way, she learns that Zareth is still alive—but he is no longer entirely human, and he does not remember his own name. Thael must decide whether to restore his memory, which might undo the Rite and unleash cosmic consequences, or leave him in peace and let the forest slowly die.
